topic: Petition of Problem Gambling Foundation of New Zealand: Prohibit the advertising of gambling
description: Petition of Problem Gambling Foundation of New Zealand: Prohibit the advertising of gambling Submitters Problem Gambling Foundation of New Zealand: Melissa Thompson, Chief Executive Andree Froude, Advocacy & Public Health Director Department of Internal Affairs: Kelly Miller, Gambling Policy Manager Jayne Beggs, Policy Director Vicki Scott, Director Gambling Regulator Gaming Machine Association of New Zealand: Peter Dengate Thrush, Independent Chair Lotto NZ: Jason Delamore, Chief Executive Leah Neilson, Chief Marketing Officer Will Hine, Head of Corporate Communications TAB NZ: Nick Roberts, Chief Executive Officer Wendie Harvey, Deputy Chair

Um, we are calling for a complete prohibition on gambling advertising in New Zealand across all forms of gambling and on all platforms. I guess we're ahead to issue a bit of a challenge today. If you've set your views on gambling harm based on land-based gambling, it's time for a reset. Our growing concern about gambling advertising stems from a rapid surge in online and sports betting, advertising the global digitalization of gambling and the ification of entertainment.

In a more general sense, our young New Zealanders are growing up in a world world where sports games and even paper elections are betting events, they're exposed at every turn through loop boxes and online games, lotto shops as they enter the supermarket, spinning a wheel for discounts when they're shopping online or in game betting.

Odds delivered by sporting heroes through a national sporting event, gambling has been normalized without adequate recognition of the inherent risk and harm. The current and emerging regulatory net around gambling is inconsistent and in places conflicting. We return profits for community good through harmful products.

We bank ban pokies from being promoted beyond venue signage at the same time as allowing highly forms of gambling like online casinos and sports betting to be aggressively advertised. The gambling act seek to control growth while our state owned operators advertised to grow profits to return to community.

We recognize gambling is harmful with one hand and encourage young gamblers with the other. Today I'm gonna talk a bit about why advertising causes harm, but my particular focus is on young people. Um, we wanna take a look at some of the, um, arguments and, and, um, positions that were shared through other submissions and just provide some context for that.

I suppose industry says there's no solid link between advertising and harm. The evidence says otherwise. Advertising is not neutral. It's designed to stimulate more frequent gambling, increase spend, and create positive associations with gambling products. Full stop. A Lancet Review found repeated exposure to gambling ads increases uptake, normalizes the behavior and drives harm in Australia.

People who saw gambling ads weekly and more were twice as likely to place impulse bets with the effects strongest in 18 to 34 year olds in Spain. When advertising and bonus restrictions were introduced recently, both new accounts and total money bet fell directly linked to those restrictions.

We hear and I say we across all of the country and across all of the forms of gambling and across all our priority populations. Hear stories of people triggered back into gambling through direct marketing in their inboxes, impossible to resist inducements and a barrage of gambling content served up as social media.

Yesterday we heard a story of a young man who can no longer watch live sporting broadcast because of in-game promotions and live betting opportunities, literally no longer able to watch live Sport wants to watch it, he has to watch it on replay. Gambling ads are literally wrestling our love of sport out of safe hands.

Some say ads don't affect kids, that's misleading. Um, in submissions, it relies on the 23 gambling survey that asked the question, if any under eighteens had ever made an unplanned bet because of an ad, it misses the point. Children can't legally place bets The real harms the early exposure that's caused.

And Wellington, the kids can study. With almost all social media content ads triggered triggering positive emotional responses in young people, this matters because adolescent brains are wired differently. Reward systems develop early while impulse control develops late. That means advertising and influencer driven promotions hit young people the hardest.

They feel stronger urges and have less ability to resist. I think we all know that. Uh, they get a sense of belonging from being the insider sentiments that are served up in jokes, niche information, special language with research showing high rates of youth, following an subscription to tips to influences and platforms.

And when I say young, I don't mean 18 year olds. We know of many, many cases of under eighteens betting at school and even fights over gambling debts and playgrounds. Exposure is happening and it's sticking. Industry says New Zealand isn't saturated like the UK or Australia. That may be true today, but it's exactly why we must act.

Sports betting ads have already surged with arrival of tain importing Australia's saturation model. And our young people do not live in a vacuum. They're on TikTok, they're on YouTube, they're on Instagram and exposed to the same global stream of gambling promotions as their peers overseas. As one of the last OECD countries to regulate online gambling, we have been targeted by offshore operators.

Let's not repeat the mistake and be the last to control gambling advertising. Lotto and TAB say that without advertising they can't meet mandated profits. This is misleading both illegal monopolies. Everyone knows where to buy a lotto ticket or place a TAB bet advertising only stimulates more losses.

International experience proves is starting to prove that profits don't require advertising and Norway nor tipping. Cut its ads spend by 40%, then another 20% and revenue still rose slightly. And this emerging case monopoly operators are remaining viable and community funding continues.

Industry says ads are needed to channel people to licensed operators. I can assure you that there are safer tools that work. Government trust, badge badges, public registers and independent consumer education when combined with strong enforcement include blocking illegal ads, stopping payments, ISP restrictions.

These measures can effectively channel without harmful advertising. It's not necessary. Operators also point to responsible gambling messages in their ads. The evidence is clear, it couldn't be clearer. They don't work. Research shows that in some cases they even boomerang making, making gambling feel safer and increasing the urge to gamble.

Real prevention comes from independent public health messaging, not from operators branding their own promotions as responsible. The DIA themselves acknowledge the limits of self-regulation when it comes to the advertising standards authority. It's voluntary. It cannot police influencer content or digital saturation at a population scale.

Gambling advertising is not neutral information. It's engineered to grow and drive losses. Adolescents are particularly vulnerable. Their reward systems mature early while self-control lags behind exposure through influences. Fast moving promotions and social media algorithms means our young people are being groomed to see gambling as normal and harmless fun.

The evidence is consistent. Advertising increases harm, volume, tree codes and industry messaging don't adequately protect monopolies don't need ads to survive. And cha channeling can be effective without gambling, without advertising, advertising. Sorry. The choice is simple. Um, I'll briefly outline Lotto New Zealand's approach to advertising, um, and specifically how we work hard, um, to avoid targeting youth and vulnerable communities. As Jason said, we are a mass market brand and in, in a year, uh, 3 million adult New Zealanders can play our games. Um, and so with that kind of reach comes a great level of responsibility.

So to deliver on our statutory purpose, our advertising is designed to be engaging, however, that has to always be balanced with our commitment to harm minimization. And over the past five years, our marketing spend has remained stable, adjusted only for inflation, and has declined as a percentage of total sales. This reflects our disciplined and social responsibility approach.

Central to our strategy are our harm minimization guidelines, which govern everything from creative development through to media placement. These are mandatory and every piece of promotional material is reviewed by a cross-functional subcommittee with escalation to executive leadership if needed. We go well beyond what is required under the advertising standards code and the gambling act to avoid targeting youth and priority communities.

And a few examples of this are we design and place advertising to appeal to audiences age 25 and over, not just 18, and certainly not youth. In fact, our games are not designed to appeal to youth at all. Every television ad is, um, checked to ensure it avoids content that appeals to younger audiences or vulnerable groups.

For example, we never advertise in shows like or other TEO programming. We don't advertise on radio stations like Flavor or My Fem. And instead we donate that ear time to charity. We avoid billboards in areas of high deprivation. So that's, um, areas nine and 10. Um, unless the message is about responsible gambling, we exclude outdoor advertising and regions with high representation of priority populations such as Northland, south Auckland, rota, poa.

We've self-imposed strict frequency caps. Traditionally, we used to sometimes place one ad, uh, three ads and just one commercial break. Now we have strict measures in place to ensure we would only show up a maximum of twice, um, in an hour. Uh, and likewise, we've heard a lot about online advertising and we, like all New Zealanders, are very rare, are aware of how much that has escalated in recent times.

We also impose strict frequency caps so that we would only have one advert, um, per person per day in those environments. And in 2020 we launched Play Smart, which is our responsible gambling initiative. It's designed to educate all players, not just those at risk around how to play safely. And it's backed by research that informed players do make better choices.

Messaging is across all of our advertising and elevated during high risk periods, such as when the Powerball jackpot is high. And for example, we we share messages such as if you've lost before, you are not likely to win, uh, next time or, um, if hi jackpots, um, still have the small odds. Finally, I want to be clear about what we don't do.

We do not use social media influences or affiliates to sell our products. We don't offer cash bonuses to entice, um, or retain players. And any promotions that we do are limited and tightly managed sponsorships require chief executives sign off and must align with our values. For example, our partnership with the New Zealand Olympic Committee, which is grounded in our longstanding connection with Sport New Zealand.

And we don't participate in large cultural events where audiences may be at greater risk, for example, Pacifica or um, Auckland Lantern Festival. So our advertising practices reflect a deep commitment to responsibility, transparency, and care for our communities. And now to pass over to Will who will share how this commitment translates to the gambling harm statistics for our products.
